beats.narration: Radiation comes in two families: particles with mass, and photons of pure energy. One shapes your safety — the other makes your image.

payload.title: TWO FAMILIES
payload.sub: The two kinds of radiation every radiographer must keep straight.

beats.narration: First, the particle family: alpha, heavy, charge plus two; beta, a fast electron, minus one; the neutron, heavy but with no charge.
Paper stops alpha. Thin aluminum stops beta. Hydrogen-rich material slows neutrons. But photons? Lead only thins them — no thickness stops every photon.

payload.heading: Penetrating power, compared
payload.note: SHIELDING THINS PHOTONS — NEVER TO ZERO

beats.narration: At equal energy, X ray and gamma photons are identical — massless, chargeless. The only difference is birthplace: gamma from the nucleus, X rays from machines.
So the rule: particles stop; photons only thin, never to zero. That penetration is why photons are the working radiation of radiography.

payload.sub: Penetration is why this method exists.
